question_text:
From the following Receipts & Payments Account data, determine the subscription income for Income & Expenditure Account:
Subscriptions received ₹4,00,000
Subscriptions outstanding — beginning ₹25,000, end ₹35,000
Subscriptions received in advance — beginning ₹10,000, end ₹18,000
explanation: Subscription for I&E = Received – Opening O/S – Closing Advance + Closing O/S + Opening Advance = ₹4,00,000 – ₹25,000 – ₹18,000 + ₹35,000 + ₹10,000 = ₹4,02,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Cash received is not the same as accrual income. Adjustments are needed.
Check adjustment directions: deduct opening O/S and closing advance; add closing O/S and opening advance.
You may have added everything instead of deducting opening O/S and closing advance.

question_text: Which of the following statements about R&P and I&E Account is CORRECT?
explanation: I&E Account is a nominal account (like P&L). R&P Account is a real account (like Cash Book). I&E records only current-year revenue items on accrual basis. R&P records all cash items regardless of period or nature.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both R&P and I&E are prepared on accrual basis
Both R&P and I&E show only revenue items
R&P is on cash basis; I&E is on accrual basis
R&P shows surplus/deficit; I&E shows cash balance

elimination_hints.hint: R&P is on cash basis, not accrual.
R&P includes both capital and revenue items.
It's the opposite — R&P shows cash balance; I&E shows surplus/deficit.

question_text: A club received ₹3,00,000 as Building Fund Donation. It purchased land for ₹2,50,000. The treatment in NPO accounts is:
explanation: Building Fund Donation is a specific fund — shown on B/S liabilities side (₹3,00,000). Land purchased is a fixed asset on B/S assets side (₹2,50,000). Neither appears in I&E Account. The fund balance (₹3L) and asset (₹2.5L) both appear on the B/S.
subject: accountancy

options.text: ₹3L income in I&E; ₹2.5L expense in I&E
₹3L added to Capital Fund; Land shown as asset
₹3L as Building Fund on B/S liabilities; Land ₹2.5L as asset on B/S

elimination_hints.hint: Specific fund items bypass I&E completely.
The ₹50K difference stays in the Building Fund, not I&E surplus.
Specific donation goes to its own fund, NOT Capital Fund.
elimination_hints.misconception: Merging specific fund with Capital Fund.

question_text: Statement: Entrance fees, if the amount is large and non-recurring, should be capitalised (added to Capital Fund).
explanation: This is TRUE. As per NCERT treatment, entrance fees of large amounts that are non-recurring in nature should be capitalised — i.e., added directly to the Capital Fund on the Balance Sheet. Small, recurring entrance fees are treated as revenue income in I&E.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Large, non-recurring entrance fees are capital. Only small, recurring ones are revenue.
elimination_hints.misconception: Always treating entrance fees as revenue.

question_text: Match Fund income with Sports Fund expenses. Sports Fund ₹1,00,000 (opening). Sports Fund income ₹30,000. Sports expenses ₹45,000. Any excess of sports expenses over income is:
explanation: Sports Fund income ₹30,000. Sports expenses ₹45,000. Excess expenses = ₹45,000 – ₹30,000 = ₹15,000. This excess is debited to Sports Fund (reduces it). Closing Sports Fund = ₹1,00,000 + ₹30,000 – ₹45,000 = ₹85,000. The ₹15,000 excess is either debited to the Sports Fund itself OR transferred to I&E Account (depending on question instruction).
subject: accountancy

options.text: Debited to Sports Fund on Balance Sheet (fund reduces to ₹85,000)
Shown as expense in I&E Account always

elimination_hints.hint: By default, excess expenses are absorbed by the specific fund. Transfer to I&E only if specifically stated.
The fund does absorb it, but the balance changes — it's not 'ignored'.
Specific fund transactions don't affect Capital Fund directly.
elimination_hints.misconception: Mixing specific fund with Capital Fund.

question_text: Salary paid during the year includes ₹20,000 for the previous year's outstanding salary. This ₹20,000 is:
explanation: The ₹20,000 was an outstanding expense of the PREVIOUS year and was already charged to last year's I&E Account. It was paid this year (appears in R&P), but should NOT be charged again in the current year's I&E Account.
subtopic: Previous year outstanding paid this year
subject: accountancy

options.text: Charged as expense in current year I&E Account
Excluded from current year I&E (was last year's expense)
Shown as an asset on Balance Sheet

elimination_hints.hint: It was already an expense last year. Double-charging would overstate this year's expenses.
Previous year's salary payment has no relation to Capital Fund.
A paid expense is not an asset. It was a previous year liability now settled.

question_text: Books purchased during the year ₹80,000. Opening stock of books ₹3,00,000. Depreciation on books 10%. Closing value of books (Library Books) on B/S is:
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹2,70K = ₹3L – 10%. You depreciated only opening, not total (opening + purchases).
₹3,80K = ₹3L + ₹80K. You forgot depreciation.
₹3,50K doesn't match. 10% of ₹3,80K = ₹38K. Closing = ₹3,42K.

question_text: A ______ is a gift received by an NPO under the will of a deceased person.
explanation: A LEGACY is a gift received under a will (testament) of a deceased person. It is a capital receipt for the NPO and is either added to Capital Fund or a specific fund (if so specified in the will).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Donation is a voluntary gift from a living person. Legacy is specifically from a will.
Grant is financial aid from government/institution. Legacy is from a deceased person's will.
Endowment is a permanent fund. Legacy is a one-time gift from a will.

question_text: Rent received ₹1,20,000 during the year. Rent receivable at beginning ₹15,000. Rent receivable at end ₹20,000. Rent received in advance at beginning ₹8,000. Rent received in advance at end ₹12,000. Rent income for I&E is:
explanation: Rent for I&E = Received – Opening receivable + Closing receivable + Opening advance – Closing advance = ₹1,20,000 – ₹15,000 + ₹20,000 + ₹8,000 – ₹12,000 = ₹1,21,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹1,20K is cash received. Adjustments for outstanding and advance are needed.
Verify each adjustment direction carefully.

question_text:
Assertion (A): Capital receipts and capital payments are not shown in the Income & Expenditure Account.
Reason (R): The I&E Account deals only with revenue items of the current period to determine surplus or deficit.
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. The I&E Account (like P&L) only shows revenue items. Capital items like purchase of assets, sale of investments, legacy, and life membership fees go directly to the Balance Sheet.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R directly explains WHY capital items are excluded — I&E only deals with revenue.
R is factually correct — I&E does deal only with revenue items.
A is correct — capital items are indeed excluded from I&E.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking capital items appear in I&E.

question_text: Government grant received by a hospital (NPO) for general purposes is treated as:
explanation: A government grant for general purposes (not earmarked) is treated as revenue income and shown in the Income & Expenditure Account. If it were for a specific purpose (like building), it would be a capital receipt shown on B/S.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Capital receipt added to Capital Fund
Shown as a liability on Balance Sheet
Not recorded — it is a government contribution

elimination_hints.hint: Only specific-purpose grants are capitalised. General grants are revenue income.
General-purpose grants are income, not liabilities.
All grants must be recorded — they are income for the NPO.

question_text: Honorarium paid to the secretary ₹24,000. Honorarium outstanding at end ₹6,000. Honorarium prepaid at end ₹3,000. Honorarium expense for I&E Account is:
explanation: Honorarium for I&E = Paid + Closing Outstanding – Closing Prepaid = ₹24,000 + ₹6,000 – ₹3,000 = ₹27,000. (No opening adjustments mentioned.)
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹24K is cash paid. Outstanding and prepaid adjustments are needed.
₹30K = ₹24K + ₹6K. You forgot to deduct prepaid.
₹21K = ₹24K – ₹3K. You forgot to add outstanding.

question_text: Sale of old sports equipment for ₹15,000 (original cost ₹40,000, written down value ₹20,000) results in:
explanation: WDV ₹20,000. Sale ₹15,000. Loss on sale = ₹20,000 – ₹15,000 = ₹5,000. This loss is debited (expense) in the I&E Account. The asset is removed from B/S at WDV and the sale proceeds are received as cash.
subject: accountancy

options.text: No effect — only shown in R&P

elimination_hints.hint: Compare sale price (₹15K) with WDV (₹20K). Sale < WDV = Loss.
₹25K = ₹40K – ₹15K. Use WDV (₹20K), not original cost (₹40K) for loss calculation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using original cost instead of WDV.

elimination_hints.hint: The cash receipt goes to R&P, but the loss/profit is shown in I&E.

question_text: Statement: If a question says 'Capitalise 50% of entrance fees', then 50% goes to Capital Fund and 50% goes to I&E Account as income.
explanation: This is TRUE. When the question specifies partial capitalisation, the specified percentage (50%) is added to Capital Fund on B/S, and the remaining (50%) is treated as revenue income in the I&E Account.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: The question explicitly states 50% capitalisation — follow the instruction for split treatment.

question_text: Interest on fixed deposit ₹40,000 accrued but not received at year-end should be:
explanation: Since I&E is on accrual basis, interest earned but not yet received (accrued interest) is recognized as income in I&E and shown as 'Accrued Interest' (asset/receivable) on the Balance Sheet's assets side.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Income in I&E and asset (Accrued Interest) on B/S

elimination_hints.hint: I&E is accrual-based. Income is recognized when earned, not when received.
R&P only records actual cash receipts. Accrued interest has no cash yet.
Accrued interest is income, not a deduction from funds.

question_text: Total members 1,000. Annual subscription ₹500. During the year: 50 members paid for next year in advance; 80 members did not pay; 30 members paid arrears of previous year. Subscription income for I&E Account is:
explanation: Total subscription due for current year = 1,000 × ₹500 = ₹5,00,000. On accrual basis, I&E shows the full amount due for the current year regardless of collection. The 80 unpaid appear as outstanding (asset), 50 advance as liability, and 30 arrears as previous year collection. I&E income = ₹5,00,000.
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: ₹4,60K = (1000 – 80) × ₹500. On accrual, ALL 1,000 members' dues are income. Unpaid = outstanding.
Current year income = 1,000 × ₹500 = ₹5L. Advance and arrears don't change this.
Advance (₹25K) and arrears (₹15K) are for other periods — not added to current year.

question_text: Locker rent, Hall rent, and interest on investments are examples of:
explanation: These are all REVENUE income items for an NPO. They are recurring, regular income earned during the course of the organisation's activities. They appear on the income (credit) side of the I&E Account.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Revenue income shown in I&E Account
Items appearing only in R&P Account

elimination_hints.hint: These are recurring, regular incomes — revenue, not capital.
These are income (receipts), not expenditure.
They appear in both R&P (cash received) and I&E (accrual income).
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking they appear only in R&P.

question_text: A school (NPO) received ₹10,00,000 as Endowment Fund. How should this be treated?
explanation: Endowment Fund is a permanent fund donated with the condition that only the INTEREST/INCOME from it can be used, not the principal. The principal (₹10L) is shown as a specific fund on the liabilities side of B/S. Interest earned goes to I&E as income.
subject: accountancy

options.text: Show as income in I&E Account
Show as Endowment Fund (separate) on liabilities side of B/S

elimination_hints.hint: Endowment is capital/permanent — only its interest is income.
Endowment is a separate fund with restrictions — not part of general Capital Fund.
Endowment principal is permanent and never consumed as income.

question_text: Statement: The Receipts & Payments Account is a REAL account while the Income & Expenditure Account is a NOMINAL account.
explanation: This is TRUE. R&P is a real account (records assets — cash/bank). It follows the rule: Debit what comes in, Credit what goes out. I&E is a nominal account (records income and expenses). It follows: Debit all expenses, Credit all incomes.
subtopic: Nature of R&P and I&E accounts
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: R&P deals with cash (real). I&E deals with income/expenses (nominal). Both classifications are correct.

question_text:
Match the NPO items with their correct treatment:

Column I:
(i) Legacy
(ii) General Donation (small)
(iii) Specific Donation (Building Fund)
(iv) Annual Subscription

Column II:
(P) Revenue income in I&E Account
(Q) Capital receipt — Capital Fund on B/S
(R) Capital receipt — Specific Fund on B/S
(S) Revenue income in I&E Account
explanation: Legacy → Capital receipt, added to Capital Fund (Q). General Donation → Revenue income in I&E (P). Specific Donation → Capital receipt, Specific Fund on B/S (R). Annual Subscription → Revenue income in I&E (S).
subject: accountancy

elimination_hints.hint: Legacy is a capital receipt (Q), not revenue income (P).
Legacy goes to Capital Fund (Q), not specific fund (R). Specific donation goes to specific fund (R).
General donation is revenue (P), not (S). Annual subscription is revenue (S).
